coredata.dc:title: Respiratory exposure to graphene quantum dots causes fibrotic effects on lung, liver and kidney of mice
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Tianshu
Wang, Xinyu
Chen, Min
Zhang, Xiaomeng
Zhang, Jixiang
Cheng, Jin
Kong, Lu
Tang, Meng
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a novel graphene-based nanoparticle, presented a bright prospect in fields of biomedicine due to their excellent optical property. However, the biosafety assessment of GQDs is far behind their rapid development, which could restrict their wilder applications. This study focused on the potential adverse effects of two kinds of promising GQDs, i.e. nitrogen-doping graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and amino-modified graphene quantum dot (A-GQDs) on primary target organs of GNMs, including lung, liver and kidney. The intranasal instillation used here was to imitate the respiratory exposure of GQDs that is a commonly exposure route of GQDs in the environment. Although no severe damages associated with general health occurred in mice treated with GQDs, the fibrosis evidenced by statistically significant increases in the area of collagen I and TGF-ß1 and p-Smad3 expressions were observed in the lung, liver and kidney tissues. Interestingly, the fibrotic effect induced by GQDs could be effectively alleviated by a ferroptosis-specific inhibitor, which demonstrated a close relationship of fibrosis and ferroptosis. This study not only provides new insights on the toxicity mechanisms of GQDs, but also offers some efficient ways to control toxicity of GQDs, like dosage threshold and small molecular drugs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doping graphene quantum dot
Amino-modified graphene quantum dot
Intranasal administration
Fibrosis
Iron overload
Lipid peroxidation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid misctext orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: GQDs caused dysfunction and histopathological alternations in lung, liver and kidney of mice
GQDs induced fibrostic effects in lung, liver and kidney of mice
GQDs disrupted the iron balance and redox equilibrium in lung, liver and kidney of mice
Ferroptosis inhibitor alleviated fibrosis caused by GQDs in the lung, liver and kidney of mice

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 82103884 , 21876026 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant number BK20180371 ) and Supporting Program of Southeast University Zhishan Young Scholar (grant number 2242021R41139 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The general toxicity of GQDs in mice. (a) The changes in body weight of mice; The changes in organ coefficient of mice lung (b), liver (c) and kidney (d). Each mouse was intranasally instilled with saline, 0.1 and 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs or A-GQDs every other day for 28 days. Data are showed as mean ± SD of three independent experiments. The one-way ANOVA followed by the Dunnett's t-test were used to determine statistical significance (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 vs the control).

The histopathological effects of GQDs in mice. Representative histological images presented the lung (a), liver (b) and kidney (c) stained with H&E. Red arrow indicates renal interstitial vasodilation and congestion. Scale bar: 100 μm for lung and kidney tissues, 50 μm for liver tissues. Each mouse was intranasally instilled with saline, 0.1 and 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs or A-GQDs every other day for 28 days. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The increased collagen I caused by GQDs in mice. Representative histological images presented the lung (a), liver (b) and kidney (c) stained using ponceau 2R-acid fuchsin-aniline blue (Masson trichrome staining). Yellow arrows indicate collagenous fibers that are stained as blue, while muscle fibers are stained as red. Scale bar: 100 μm. (d) The collagenvolume fraction (CVF) of each group in the lung, liver and kidney. Each mouse was intranasally instilled with saline, 0.1 and 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs or A-GQDs every other day for 28 days. Data are showed as mean ± SD of three independent experiments. The one-way ANOVA followed by the Dunnett's t-test were used to determine statistical significance (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 vs the control). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The fibrosis marker altered by GQDs in mice. Representative immunohistochemical images presented p-Smad3 in the lung (a), liver (b) and kidney (c). Black arrows indicate nuclear translocation of p-Smad3. Scale bar: 100 μm. (d) The IHC score of each group in the lung, liver and kidney. (e) The alternations on the expressions of proteins p-Smad3, Smad3 and TGF-ß1 in the lung, liver and kidney. Each mouse was intranasally instilled with saline, 0.1 and 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs or A-GQDs every other day for 28 days. Data are showed as mean ± SD of three independent experiments. The one-way ANOVA followed by the Dunnett's t-test were used to determine statistical significance (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 vs the control).

The iron overload and redox imbalance caused by GQDs in mice. The levels of total iron, ferrous iron (Fe2+) and ferric iron (Fe3+) (a), MDA content (b) and NADP+/NADPH ratio (c) in the lung, liver and kidney; (d) The alternations on the expressions of ferroptosis-relevant proteins GPX4, SLC7A11 and ACSL4 in the lung, liver and kidney. Each mouse was intranasally instilled with saline, 0.1 and 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs or A-GQDs every other day for 28 days. Data are showed as mean ± SD of three independent experiments. The one-way ANOVA followed by the Dunnett's t-test were used to determine statistical significance (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 vs the control).

Ferrostatin-1 reversed the collagen I increased by GQDs in mice. Representative histological images presented the lung (a), liver (b) and kidney (c) stained using ponceau 2R-acid fuchsin-aniline blue (Masson trichrome staining). Yellow arrows indicate collagenous fibers that are stained as blue, while muscle fibers are stained as red. Scale bar: 100 μm. (d) The collagenvolume fraction (CVF) of each group in the lung, liver and kidney. Each mouse was intranasally instilled with saline and 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs or A-GQDs every other day for 28 days. For Fer-1 treatment groups, each mouse was intraperitoneally injected with 1 mg/kg BW Fer-1 every fourth day and intranasally instilled with 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs or A-GQDs every other day for 28 days. The one-way ANOVA followed by the Dunnett's t-test were used to determine statistical significance (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 vs the control or N-GQDs/A-GQDs treatment). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Ferrostatin-1 reversed the fibrosis marker altered by GQDs in mice. Representative immunohistochemical images presented p-Smad3 in the lung (a), liver (b) and kidney (c). Black arrows indicate nuclear translocation of p-Smad3. Scale bar: 100 μm. (d) The IHC score of each group in the lung, liver and kidney. (e) The alternations on the expressions of proteins p-Smad3 and Smad3 in the lung, liver and kidney. Each mouse was intranasally instilled with saline and 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs or A-GQDs every other day for 28 days. For Fer-1 treatment groups, each mouse was intraperitoneally injected with 1 mg/kg BW Fer-1 every fourth day and intranasally instilled with 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs or A-GQDs every other day for 28 days. The one-way ANOVA followed by the Dunnett's t-test were used to determine statistical significance (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 vs the control or N-GQDs/A-GQDs treatment).

head.ce:title: Respiratory exposure to graphene quantum dots causes fibrotic effects on lung, liver and kidney of mice

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education, School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing, 210009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education; School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, P.R.China.

head.ce:miscellaneous: Handling Editor: Dr. Jose Luis Domingo

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a novel graphene-based nanoparticle, presented a bright prospect in fields of biomedicine due to their excellent optical property. However, the biosafety assessment of GQDs is far behind their rapid development, which could restrict their wilder applications. This study focused on the potential adverse effects of two kinds of promising GQDs, i.e. nitrogen-doping graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and amino-modified graphene quantum dot (A-GQDs) on primary target organs of GNMs, including lung, liver and kidney. The intranasal instillation used here was to imitate the respiratory exposure of GQDs that is a commonly exposure route of GQDs in the environment. Although no severe damages associated with general health occurred in mice treated with GQDs, the fibrosis evidenced by statistically significant increases in the area of collagen I and TGF-ß1 and p-Smad3 expressions were observed in the lung, liver and kidney tissues. Interestingly, the fibrotic effect induced by GQDs could be effectively alleviated by a ferroptosis-specific inhibitor, which demonstrated a close relationship of fibrosis and ferroptosis. This study not only provides new insights on the toxicity mechanisms of GQDs, but also offers some efficient ways to control toxicity of GQDs, like dosage threshold and small molecular drugs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a novel type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that can be used in wide fields because of their excellent properties relevant to both graphene and QDs, such as good biocompatibility and bright fluorescence (Zhang et al., 2019). So far, multiple chemical functionalizations have been conducted by researchers to investigate economical and green strategies to synthesize GQDs that are suitable for various applications (Du and Guo, 2016; Kaur et al., 2018b; Wang et al., 2020). Since the toxicity of graphene have been extensively evaluated during the past decades, it is vigilant for researchers to pay attention on the potential toxic effects of GQDs in living organisms due to their inherent graphene-like property (De Marchi et al., 2018; Makvandi et al., 2020; Yao et al., 2019). Among a variety of GQDs, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (A-GQDs) recently present promising value in the field of biomedicine, such as imaging technique, drug delivery and photophysical therapy (Chen et al., 2016; Kaur et al., 2018b), so the lack of information on bio-safety assessment would put restriction on their bio-applications. Meanwhile, along with the increasing applications of N-GQDs and A-GQDs, their exposure to the public and the environment undoubtedly put a risk on the health of human beings. Up to now, there is little information on the adverse impacts of N-GQDs and A-GQDs on the living bodies after inhalation that is a common exposure route of nanomaterials in the environment, as well as on the similarities and differences of GQDs functionalized with different chemical modifications, i.e. nitrogen-doping or amino-functionlization.

So far, it still has been controversial whether GQDs exert toxic effects in living organisms. Some studies reported the low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of GQDs (Fang et al., 2018; Xuan et al., 2018), but the knowledge was mainly obtained from in vitro rather than more persuasive in vivo toxicity assessment. During recent years, the potential toxicity of GQDs is getting more and more attention from researchers, and GQDs were found to cause oxidative stress damages, such as disrupting redox-sensitive system (Deng et al., 2019), producing excessive ROS (Campbell et al., 2021; Kuo et al., 2018), genotoxic damages (Ku et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2019), and abnormalities in locomotor activities (Yan et al., 2020). As we know, the respiratory system is a main exposure route of nanomaterials that are in work environment and consumer products to the general public (Ema et al., 2017; Pelin et al., 2018), so it is a matter of great concern to conduct safety evaluations on the potential health risks of airway exposed GQDs. Since lung, liver and kidney have been reported to be main targets of respiratory exposed nanomaterials in several studies (Nho, 2020; Osman et al., 2020; Poh et al., 2018), this study focused on the possible impairments of lung, liver and kidney of mice intranasally exposed to N-GQDs and A-GQDs for 28 days.

In this study, the exposure dosages are according to the bio-applied dosages of N-GQDs or A-GQDs in several laboratory experiments and our pre-experiments, because this novel kind of nanoparticle has not yet been licensed in the clinic applications and their environmental relevant exposure concentrations are scarce (Henna and Pramod, 2020b; Mansuriya and Altintas, 2020; Tade et al., 2020). Even though both N-GQDs and A-GQDs did not cause severe damages in the mainly targeted organs of nanomaterials, i.e. lung, liver and kidney, the slight fibrotic effects induced by GQDs should be attached importance to by researchers because the consequences of fibrosis were severe. One interesting thing is that the fibrotic alternations in lung, liver and kidney caused by GQDs could be alleviated by a specific ferroptosis inhibitor called ferrostatin-1 (Fer-1). Ferroptosis is a programmed cell death mediated by iron overload and lipid peroxidation (Dixon et al., 2012; Jiang et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020), and it has been reported in several studies the pulmonary, hepatic, and renal fibrosis are attributed to the disruption of iron and lipid metabolisms (Jia et al., 2021; Mehta et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2021). It seems that ferroptosis could be involved in the fibrosis induced by GQDs, which provides an interesting topic for further studies.

The p-Smad3, Smad3, TGF-ß1 and GAPDH primary antibodies, and secondary antibody HRP conjugated anti-rabbit IgG were all purchased from ABclonal Technology (Wuhan, China). Iron Assay Kit with Colorimetric was purchased from Dojindo Molecular Technology (Japan). NADP+/NADPH Assay Kit with WST-8 and Lipid Peroxidation Malondialdehyde (MDA) Assay Kit were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Ferrostain-1 (Fer-1) was purchased from APExBIO (TX, USA).

The N-GQDs (Product No. XF241) and the A-GQDs (Product No. XF093) used in this study were purchased from XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China) (http://www.xfnano.com), and their physicochemical characterizations were evaluated before the study. High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) images were acquired on an electron microscope. Individual N-GQDs and A-GQDs were uniform with an average particle size of approximately 3 nm and 4 nm, respectively (Figs. S1a and S1b). The particle morphology was examined by atomic force microscopy (AFM). The thicknesses of individual N-GQDs were approximately 1.6 nm and the thicknesses of individual A-GQDs were from 3.5 to 8.5 nm (Figs. S1c and S1d). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) and surface ξ-potential measurements of N-GQDs and A-GQDs in DI water and saline were carried out on a Malcern Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Fig. S1e∼S1h). The absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs and A-GQDs in DI water and saline were measured on a UV-2550 spectrometer and in Figure S1i∼S1l. All data were also showed in Table S1.

Total 42 male ICR mice (6 mice per group) aged 8 weeks were purchased from Zhejiang Academy of Medical Sciences (Zhejiang, China). All mice were housed in stainless steel cages in a ventilated animal facility at a temperature maintained in 22 ± 2 °C and relative humidity of 65 ± 10% under a 12 h light/dark cycle, and feed with sterilized food and distilled water. All mice were treated humanely throughout the experimental period. All animal procedures were performed in strict accordance with the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of Southeast University and experiments were approved by the Animal Experimental Ethics Committee of Southeast University (Nanjing, China).

In N-GQDs- or A-GQDs-treatment groups, each animal was administered with 0.1 or 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs or A-GQDs through intranasal instillation for 28 alternate days, while the control were exposed to normal saline solution with the same administration method and time. In Fer-1-treatment groups, each animal was administered with 1 mg/kg BW Fer-1 through intraperitoneal injection every fourth day for 28 days, and then they were intranasally exposed to 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs or A-GQDs or saline every second day. The detailed administration procedure showed in Fig. S2.

All mice were weighted throughout the whole exposure period and then sacrificed through inhaling carbon dioxide at the end of exposure. Blood of each animal was collected and centrifugated into plasma to do the biochemical test using an auto-analyzer (Hitachi 7060, Hitachi, Tokyo). The lungs, livers and kidneys were quickly removed and weighted. A part of these tissues was fixed in 4% (w/v) paraformaldehyde for histological and immunohistochemistry (IHC) analyses. Meanwhile, some dissected lungs, livers and kidneys were stored in liquid nitrogen for ELISA assays (Iron assay, Lipid Peroxidation MDA Assay and NADP+/NADPH Assay) and western blotting analysis.

The lung, liver and kidney sections were incubated with p-Smad3 primary antibodies at 1:100 dilution overnight at 4 °C. After that, slices were incubated with biotinylated anti-rabbit IgG secondary antibody at 1:500 dilution followed with DAB staining for signal development. All sections were visualized under a light microscope and images were taken. Images were analyzed by the IHC profiler in ImageJ software (version 1.53, https://imagej.nih.gov/ij/) to calculate the IHC score of each group based on randomly selected ten images. The analysis procedure is following by the work flow described in the study of Varghese F et al.(Varghese et al., 2014). Briefly, IHC score is based on the average grey value of positive cells (staining intensity) and the percentage of positive area (staining area) in IHC images. The IHC scores include High Positive scores 4, Positive scores 3, Low Positive scores 2 and negative scores 1.

The levels of total iron, ferrous iron and ferric iron in lungs, livers and kidneys were determined by using the commercial Iron Assay Kit with Colorimetric. The MDA contents in lungs, livers and kidneys were measured using the commercial lipid peroxidation MDA assay kit. The levels of NADP and NADPH, and their ratio in lungs, livers and kidneys were measured using the commercial NADP+/NADPH Assay Kit with WST-8. All treatments were performed in triplicate in three independent experiments according to the manufacturer's instructions.

The expression levels of proteins (Smad3, p-Smad3 and TGF-ß1) in lungs, livers and kidneys were assessed by western blotting analysis after extracting total protein. The densitometry analysis of WB results was using ImageJ software (version 1.53, https://imagej.nih.gov/ij/) to get grey values of each bend. All protein levels were normalized to reference protein GAPDH, and p-Smad3 protein levels were also normalized to Smad3. The same samples ran three times and the experiment was repeated independently at least three times.

All data were displayed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis was performed using Graphpad Prism 6.0 Software. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to determine the statistical significance between the control and exposed groups followed with the Dunnett's t-test to determine the significance of differences between groups. Probability levels of <0.05, <0.01 and < 0.001were considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs caused dysfunction and histopathological alternations in lung, liver and kidney of mice
ce:section.ce:para: Owning to the bright fluorescence of N-GQDs and A-GQDs, the distribution and accumulation of GQDs in main organs of mice, including heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidneys were detected using an in vivo fluorescence imaging system for small animals. According to the fluorescent intensity measured as radiant efficiency in tissues, the fluorescence in liver and kidney tissues exposed to N-GQDs and A-GQDs were obviously stronger than the control (Fig. S3). Similar to reported studies showing airway exposure graphene-based nanomaterials (GNMs) could distribute to whole body (Ema et al., 2017), it seems that the liver and kidneys might be mainly toxic targets of GQDs in the circulatory system coming from airway exposure, on which should be emphasized. Meanwhile, the reason of no obvious increases in the fluorescent intensity of lung might be attributed to the strong lung clearance ability, but we still assessed the possible impairments in lung because the respiratory system is an undoubtedly primary target of inhaled nanomaterials.

During the whole study, no severely side-effects including mice death occurred. Although the body weight of mice in GQDs-treated groups were not statistically significantly changed compared to the control (Fig. 1 a), slight changes in organ coefficient of lung, liver and kidney were observed in N-GQDs- or A-GQDs-exposed mice or both (Fig. 1b∼1d). Meanwhile, the biomedical indicators, including alanine aminotransferase (ALT), alkaline phosphatase (AKP) and urea, in blood plasma of mice treated with A-GQDs were statistically significantly altered to different degrees when comparing with the control to show mild injury of liver and kidney (Table S2). When we assessed histopathological sections of lung, liver and kidney tissues, some mild alternations were observed in the representative images. In lungs of mice exposed to 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs and A-GQDs, slight alveolar dilatation was detected (Fig. 2 a), and slight renal interstitial vasodilation and congestion was detected in kidneys of mice exposed to 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs (Fig. 2c), while no obviously pathological changes were detected in livers of mice exposed to two types of GQDs (Fig. 2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs induced fibrostic effects in lung, liver and kidney of mice
ce:section.ce:para: Several studies have reported that the inhalation of GNMs might lead to formation of granulomas and fibrosis in lung of rodents (Sanchez et al., 2012), so the potentially fibrostic effects induced by GQDs were evaluated in this study. Since H&E staining cannot present the potential fibrosis induced by GQDs because of their slight fibrotic effects, the Masson trichrome staining was firstly used to distinguish the collagenous fibers and muscle fibers in lung, liver and kidney tissues to reflect the degree of fibrogenesis. According to the representative images, the areas of collagenous fibers in lung tissues of mice treated with two types of GQDs at the both two dosages, while in liver tissues of mice treated with 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs and in kidney tissues of mice treated with 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs and A-GQDs, areas of collagenous fibers were all obviously larger than that of muscle fibers when compared to the control (Fig. 3ã3c). Meanwhile, a semi-quantitative analysis of fibrosis using the CVF that is calculated with the assistance of ImageJ software was used and indicated that 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs exposure statistically significantly increased the collagen I in lung, liver and kidney tissues, while 1 mg/kg BW A-GQDs exposure statistically significantly increased the collagen I in lung and kidney tissues when compared to the control (Fig. 3 d).

Smad proteins have been extensively studied to regulate progressive hepatic, renal and pulmonary fibrosis through activating transforming growth factor-beta1 (TGF-ß1) (Meng et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2016). Among various proteins in the Smad family, Smad3 is pro-fibrotic in the context of fibrosis, and the phosphorylation of Smad3 followed with the nuclear translocation of p-Smad3 play an essential role in the process of fibrogenesis (Murata et al., 2014; Yoshida et al., 2018). Therefore, in this study, the expression levels of phosphorylated Smad3 (p-Smad3) in the lung, liver and kidney tissues of mice were measured using IHC staining. Representative images suggested that 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs and A-GQDs might increase the expression levels of p-Smad3 in lung and liver tissues to some degrees (Fig. 4ã4c). With the assistance of IHC profiler in ImageJ software under the model of nuclear stained image, we scored randomly selected ten IHC images of each group and the results suggested that the enhancements in the p-Smad3 protein expressions in lung and liver tissues of mice exposed to 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs and A-GQDs were statistically significant compared to the control, while no remarkably alternations of p-Smad3 expressions in kidney tissues (Fig. 4 d). Similar to the IHC staining, WB analysis demonstrated that the expression levels of p-Smad3 protein increased in lung, liver and kidney tissues when the expression levels of Smad3 protein did not change after GQDs exposure (Fig. 4e and Fig. S4). Additionally, the expression levels of TGF-ß1 also increased in lung, liver and kidney of mice exposed to GQDs (Fig. 4e and Fig. S4). All in all, the high dose exposure of GQDs were capable of inducing fibrosis in lung, liver and kidney even though the fibrotic effects of GQDs were not severe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs disrupted the iron balance and redox equilibrium in lung, liver and kidney of mice
ce:section.ce:para: Our previous study indicated the ability of N-GQDs and A-GQDs inducing ferroptosis in neuronal cells (Wu et al., 2020), it is rational to hypothesize that the two types of GQDs might also cause adverse alternations of ferroptosis, i.e. iron overload and lipid peroxidation, in lung, liver and kidney of mice as well. When measuring the iron contents, including total iron (Fe), ferrous iron (Fe2+) and ferric iron (Fe3+), 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs increased the levels of total Fe, Fe2+ and Fe3+ in both lung and kidney tissues and only increased the levels of Fe3+ in liver tissues, while 1 mg/kg BW A-GQDs enhanced the levels of Fe3+ in all lung, liver and kidney tissues (Fig. 5 a). In this study, two indexes were used to assess the lipid peroxidation caused by GQDs. Firstly, the results demonstrated that 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs and A-GQDs both elicited increase in the contents of lipid peroxidative product MDA in lung, liver and kidney tissues (Fig. 5b). Meanwhile, the ratio of NADP+ and NADPH significantly increased in lung, liver and kidney tissues of mice exposed to 1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs and A-GQDs, while the treatments of 0.1 mg/kg BW N-GQDs and A-GQDs also led to reduction in the NADPH content in kidney (Fig. 5c). NADPH is a coenzyme of GSH reductase and decreased NADPH level reflects the GSH depletion that is associated with redox homeostasis disruption (Deng et al., 2019). The results of MDA and NADPH indicated that GQDs were capable of breaking the redox equilibrium and causing lipid peroxidation in lung, liver and kidney tissues. Additionally, the changed protein expressions of three widely-reported biomarkers of ferroptosis, i.e. GPX4, SLC7A11 and ACSL4 were observed in lung, liver and kidney tissues of mice exposed to N-GQDs and A-GQDs (Fig. 5d and Fig. S5). It seems that GQDs exposed to a high dose could trigger ferroptosis in lung, liver and kidney of mice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ferroptosis inhibitor alleviated fibrosis caused by GQDs in the lung, liver and kidney of mice

ce:section.ce:para: So far, GQDs have been considered as a desirable nanomaterial in many fields due to their excellent properties (Henna and Pramod, 2020a; Mansuriya and Altintas, 2020). Along with the rapidly growing application of GQDs, their risk to the public and environment is increasing, but the information on their biosafety assessment is deficient. In this study, two valuable types of GQDs, i.e. N-GQDs and A-GQDs, were chosen to evaluate their adverse effects in the living bodies, especially primary toxic targets of nanomaterials including lung, liver and kidney. Since nanomaterials in the environment usually enter into the human beings through the respiratory system, the exposure administration here was intranasal instillation to simulate the airway exposure. Meanwhile, the lack of data on the environmentally relevant concentration of GQDs makes us no choice but to use bio-applied dosages of GQDs in laboratory experiments to assess their biosafety.

Owning to the excellent fluorescence of both N-GQDs and A-GQDs, their biodistribution and accumulation can be directly observed using an in vivo fluorescence imaging system for small animals. The fluorescent images suggested that liver and kidney tissues were main organs of storing up GQDs, which could be attributed to their biological functions closely related to absorption, metabolism and excretion, which has been found in several other nanomaterials (Huang and Tang, 2021; Wang et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2020). Otherwise, although lung is the first exposure organ of inhaled nanomaterials, the fluorescence intensity in lung tissues of mice exposed to both two kinds of GQDs did not obviously increase, which could be explained by the natural defense mechanism of lung and the tiny size of GQDs that contribute to the easy elimination of GQDs from the lung (Ngan and Asmawi, 2018). However, no observed accumulation of GQDs does not mean no damages in the lung. Therefore, the potential toxic effects of respiratory exposed N-GQDs and A-GQDs in the lung, liver and kidney of mice were investigated in this study.

Fortunately, respiratory exposed N-GQDs and A-GQDs both did not cause severe damages in the whole health of mice, but alternations of organ coefficient and biochemical indicators were observed, which indicated that the structures and functions of lung, liver and kidney could be damaged by GQDs. Firstly, histopathological changes were observed in the lung and kidney tissues of mice exposed to GQDs, especially at the high dosage, but no obvious histopathological changes in the liver tissues, which might account in the strong recovery ability of liver from nanotoxicity (Kermanizadeh et al., 2020). Recently, many studies have reported the ability of GNMs eliciting pulmonary, hepatic or renal fibrosis and fibrosis is the end result of chronic inflammatory reactions to several stimuli, such as persistent infections, chemical insults and radiation, and characterized by an excessive deposition of extracellular matrix (ECM) of predominantly type I collagen (Friedman, 2008). Since H&E staining images cannot present slight fibrosis, the fibrotic effects of GQDs in the lung, liver and kidney tissues were primarily assessed by special staining in this study. Firstly, The Masson staining images and data of CVF based on these images demonstrated an increase in collagen I in lung, liver and kidney tissues of mice exposed to GQDs, which indicated fibrosis occurred and higher exposure dosages of N-GQDs and A-GQDs resulted in severer fibrosis.

The molecular mechanisms of mediating fibrosis have been extensively assessed by many studies, and it is of particular note that TGF-1/Smad signal induces matric production in the pathogenesis of fibrosis in several organs, like liver and kidney (Border and Noble, 1998). Among Smad proteins, Smad3 and Smad4 appear to be pathogenic roles in TGF-ß1-induced fibrosis but Smad4 being working through regulating Smad3, while Smad2 and Smad7 exert opposing protective functions in regulation of fibrosis (Gong and Yang, 2020; Tang et al., 2018). Therefore, Smad3 and TGF-ß1 were chosen as the fibrotic biomarkers to assess the fibrosis induced by GQDs in this study. According to available studies, the phosphorylation of Smad3 by active TGF-1ß is pathogenic to fibrosis when p-Smad3 translocate into the nucleus to regulate the transcription of target genes of triggering collagens production, so the levels of p-Smad3 in the nucleus reflect the active intensity of TGF-1ß signal pathway-mediated fibrosis (Meng et al., 2015; Wrana et al., 1994). In this study, increases in the abundance of p-Smad3 in the nucleus and the expression level of p-Smad3 and TGF-ß1 in whole tissues were observed in the lung and liver of mice exposed to N-GQDs and A-GQDs, especially at the high dosage, which not only confirmed the pulmonary and hepatic fibrosis induced by GQDs, but also indicated the important role of Smad3 in GQDs causing fibrosis in lung and liver. However, no alternations in p-Smad3 in kidney tissues of mice exposed to GQDs suggested that the GQDs-inducing renal fibrosis is not Smad3-dependent, which should be investigated in further studies.

Even though the fibrotic effects induced by GQDs are not severe, it still needs attention from researchers when doing bio-safety assessment of GQDs because the consequences of fibrosis. When exploring the possible way to alleviate the fibrotic damages caused by GQDs, our previous study suggesting the ferroptosis caused by both N-GQDs and A-GQDs provides a hint on the potential reasons of GQDs inducing fibrosis. Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent cell death involving a lethal accumulation of lipid peroxides. Excess iron has been reported to contribute to fibrosis through generating unquenchable amounts of free radicals by Fenton reaction or inducing fibrosis-promoting signals (Mehta et al., 2019). Recently, some researchers found the critical role of ferroptosis in the progress of fibrogenesis, and the inhibition of ferroptosis could alleviate the fibrosis, especially in liver and lung (Jia et al., 2021; Stockwell et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2021). In this study, two specific characteristics of ferroptosis-related alternation, i.e. iron overload and lipid peroxidation, obviously occurred in the lung, liver and kidney of mice exposed to N-GQDs and A-GQDs, especially at the high dosage. Meanwhile, three molecular biomarkers of ferroptosis, including GPX4, SLC7A11 and ACSL4, were also used to assess the ferroptosis triggered by GQDs. GPX4 serves a key factor in reducing lipid peroxidation, and SLC7A11 as the up-stream of GPX4 is a critical component of system X− c that is responsible for maintaining redox homeostasis (Lang et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2014; Yang and Stockwell, 2016). ACSL4 is a key enzyme of regulation lipid composition and has been confirmed to contribute to ferroptosis execution (Li et al., 2019). Therefore, the inhibition on expressions of GPX4 and SLC7A11 as well as the up-regulation of ACSL4 suggested that GQDs might instigate ferroptosis through overwhelming anti-oxidative system that has been reported a key modulator of lipid peroxidation and resulting in this novel form of cell death in lung, liver and kidney tissues of mice. Furthermore, when the GQDs-caused ferroptosis were reversed by the specific ferroptosis inhibitor Fer-1, pulmonary, hepatic and renal fibrosis were all alleviated to some extents, which suggested the contribution of ferroptosis to the GQDs-induced fibrosis in internal organs.

Comprehensive analysis of all toxicity data, the intranasal administration of N-GQDs and A-GQDs at a relative low dosage reported in available studies (0.1 mg/kg BW) both do not lead to severely adverse effects in the lung, liver and kidney tissues of mice, no matter fibrotic effects or ferroptosis relevant alternations, while a high dosage (1 mg/kg BW) of N-GQDs and A-GQDs elicit obviously fibrotic damages in the lung, liver and kidney tissues of mice which could be alleviated by the specific inhibitor of ferroptosis. The findings reminded us that the control of administrated dosage is a simple way to avoid toxic effects of GQDs during their applications. Meantime, when the potential toxic mechanisms of GQDs were studied, it is also an efficient way of using specific molecular drugs to control their adverse consequences through mediating biological signals. As we known, the physicochemical properties of nanomaterials could remarkably influence their biological interaction with living organisms, so two types of GQDs functionalized with different chemical modifications were assessed here. Nitrogen-doping is an economical and green strategy in the development on applications of GQDs (Kaur et al., 2018a), while amino group modification is a common way to improve the biocompatibility of GQDs (Kuo et al., 2018). The findings indicated no discernible distinguishes between the fibrotic effects caused by the intranasal administration of N-GQDs and A-GQDs in lung, liver and kidney. Therefore, N-GQDs and A-GQDs both represent promising perspectives on the biomedical applications.

In this study, we found that the intranasal administration of N-GQDs and A-GQDs at a dosage of 1 mg/kg BW caused slightly pulmonary, hepatic and renal fibrosis, and ferroptosis in mice, which could be impeded by a ferroptosis-specific inhibitor Fer-1. However, 0.1 mg/kg BW GQDs exposure did not result in severe impairments on the lung, liver and kidney of mice. Additionally, the in vivo toxicity of N-GQDs and A-GQDs was similar at the respiratory exposure, which provides positive information on the wilder applications of these GQDs functionalized with chemical modifications. This study not only emphasizes the necessity and importance of biosafety assessment of so called low-toxic QDs, but also points out many simple and efficient routes to relax the potential adverse effects of GQDs, such as the control of exposure dosage and small molecular drug.

This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 82103884, 21876026), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant number BK20180371) and Supporting Program of Southeast University Zhishan Young Scholar (grant number 2242021R41139).

Tianshu Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, and Review & Editing. Xinyu Wang: Validation, Data curation. Min Chen: Validation, Data curation. Xiaomeng Zhang: Validation, Data curation. Jixiang Zhang: Validation. Jin Cheng: Validation. Lu Kong: Supervision. Meng Tang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
